title: The Feedback Loop: How Models Change the World
concept: A feedback loop occurs when a model's own predictions influence the future data it will later be trained on, creating a self-reinforcing cycle that can amplify bias or distort reality.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why might a team choose a slightly less accurate but much faster model for a real-time system?

options.text: Because meeting a strict latency requirement is critical, and an overly slow model can make the app feel broken.
Because faster models are always more accurate.
Because latency has no impact on real-time user experience.

exercises.explain: Correct! Meeting latency requirements can outweigh small accuracy differences in real-time systems.

cards.title: A song that gets more popular just by being recommended
cards.body: Imagine a music app recommends a decent (but not amazing) song to millions of users simply because an early data glitch made it look popular. More people play it because it was recommended. Now the model sees it's popular... and recommends it even more. The model's own prediction became a self-fulfilling prophecy.

cards.title: The Feedback Loop
cards.body: A feedback loop happens when a model's predictions directly influence the future data it will be trained on again, creating a self-reinforcing cycle — sometimes amplifying small biases into large, distorted patterns over time.

cards.title: A microphone facing its own speaker
cards.body: A feedback loop is like pointing a microphone directly at the speaker it's connected to. A tiny sound gets picked up, amplified, played back, picked up again, amplified again — quickly spiraling into an ear-splitting screech. A model's predictions influencing its own future training data can spiral in a similar self-reinforcing way.

cards.title: The predictive policing trap
cards.body: A model predicts which neighborhoods likely need more police patrols based on past arrest data. More patrols get sent there, which naturally leads to more arrests being recorded (simply because more officers are present to make them). The model then 'confirms' that neighborhood needs even more patrols — even if the actual crime rate never changed.

cards.title: A feedback loop isn't caused by a coding bug
cards.body: You might assume a feedback loop means something is technically broken in the code. In reality, every individual step (recommend a song, log a play, retrain on plays) can work perfectly and still combine into a distorted, self-reinforcing cycle — the danger comes from the system design, not a bug.

left.text:
Model recommends an item.
Users see and interact with it.

right.text:
That interaction becomes new training data.
Model 'confirms' its own earlier guess.

exercises.prompt: What is a feedback loop in machine learning?

options.text: When a model's predictions influence the future data it will be trained on, creating a self-reinforcing cycle.
A method for saving a model to a joblib file.
A technique for splitting data into train and test sets.
The process of choosing hyperparameters using grid search.

exercises.explain: Correct! Feedback loops occur when predictions shape the future training data itself.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A feedback loop always means there is a bug somewhere in the model's code.
exercises.explain: False! Every step can work exactly as designed, and the loop still emerges from the overall system design, not a code bug.

exercises.sentence: A ___ loop happens when a model's own predictions shape the future data it later learns from.
exercises.explain: A feedback loop describes predictions circling back to influence future training data.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario best illustrates a feedback loop?

options.text: A video platform recommends a video more because it has many views, which causes it to be shown to even more people, generating even more views.
A developer manually fixes a typo in the model's training script.
A team splits their dataset into a training set and a separate test set.

exercises.explain: Correct! Views driving more recommendations, which drive even more views, is a textbook feedback loop.

exercises.prompt: A job-matching platform's model shows certain job listings more often to a specific group because early data showed slightly higher click rates from that group. Over time, this group receives far more of those job recommendations, and the click data 'confirms' the pattern even further, even though other groups may have been just as interested. What is happening, and why is it risky?

options.text: A feedback loop is amplifying a small early bias into a much larger pattern over time, potentially unfairly limiting opportunities shown to other groups.
The model has a coding bug that must be immediately patched.
This is a normal, harmless pattern with no risk of amplification.

exercises.explain: Exactly! This is a real and important risk of feedback loops — small early differences can spiral into large, unfair patterns purely through the self-reinforcing cycle of predictions shaping future data. Congratulations on completing the Model Serving unit! Next, we'll bring everything together in a hands-on mini project!
